Pandowdy Apple IIe Emulator
Table of Contents

Interface IConfigurable

Namespace
Pandowdy.EmuCore.Interfaces
Assembly
Pandowdy.EmuCore.dll

Represents an object that can persist and restore its configuration state via metadata strings.

public interface IConfigurable

Examples

Simple Implementation (JSON-based): 

public class SimpleDevice : IConfigurable
{
    public string DeviceName { get; set; } = "Unnamed";
    public bool IsEnabled { get; set; } = true;

    public string GetMetadata()
    {
        try
        {
            return JsonSerializer.Serialize(new 
            { 
                DeviceName, 
                IsEnabled 
            });
        }
        catch
        {
            return string.Empty; // Fail-safe
        }
    }

    public bool ApplyMetadata(string metadata)
    {
        if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(metadata))
        {
            // Reset to defaults
            DeviceName = "Unnamed";
            IsEnabled = true;
            return true;
        }

        try
        {
            var config = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<dynamic>(metadata);
            DeviceName = config.DeviceName;
            IsEnabled = config.IsEnabled;
            return true;
        }
        catch
        {
            return false; // Invalid metadata
        }
    }
}

// Usage
var device = new SimpleDevice { DeviceName = "Serial Port", IsEnabled = false };
string metadata = device.GetMetadata();

var newDevice = new SimpleDevice();
if (newDevice.ApplyMetadata(metadata))
{
    Console.WriteLine($"Restored: {newDevice.DeviceName}");
}

Hierarchical Implementation (Controller with Drives): 

public class DiskController : IConfigurable
{
    private List<DiskDrive> _drives = new();

    public string GetMetadata()
    {
        try
        {
            var config = new
            {
                Version = 1,
                ControllerSettings = new { /* controller-specific settings */ },
                Drives = _drives.Select(d => new
                {
                    Slot = d.Slot,
                    // Include child's metadata inline
                    DriveConfig = d.GetMetadata()
                }).ToArray()
            };

            return JsonSerializer.Serialize(config);
        }
        catch
        {
            return string.Empty;
        }
    }

    public bool ApplyMetadata(string metadata)
    {
        if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(metadata))
        {
            _drives.Clear();
            return true;
        }

        try
        {
            var config = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<dynamic>(metadata);

            // Apply controller settings first
            // ... apply controller-specific configuration ...

            // Recreate drives based on embedded configuration
            _drives.Clear();
            foreach (var driveConfig in config.Drives)
            {
                var drive = new DiskDrive(driveConfig.Slot);

                // Recursively apply child metadata
                if (!drive.ApplyMetadata(driveConfig.DriveConfig.ToString()))
                {
                    return false; // Child configuration failed
                }

                _drives.Add(drive);
            }

            return true;
        }
        catch
        {
            return false;
        }
    }
}

Remarks

The IConfigurable interface provides a simple, format-agnostic mechanism for objects to serialize and deserialize their configuration state. Implementers choose their own metadata format (JSON, YAML, XML, binary, etc.) based on their complexity and requirements.

Design Philosophy:

  • Format Freedom: Each implementation chooses its own serialization format
  • Self-Contained: Metadata strings are opaque to consumers
  • Fail-Safe: ApplyMetadata(string) returns success status rather than throwing exceptions
  • Stateless Protocol: No assumptions about when/how metadata is captured or applied
  • Hierarchical Freedom: Objects may include child configurations or manage them separately

Common Use Cases:

  • Persisting peripheral card configurations (drives, settings, etc.)
  • Saving/loading emulator state snapshots
  • Cloning configured objects with preserved state
  • Exporting/importing configurations between sessions

Hierarchical Configuration:
Objects with child components (e.g., a disk controller with attached drives) have flexibility in how they handle child configuration:

  • Inclusive Approach: Parent includes child metadata within its own (recommended for tight coupling)
  • Separate Approach: Parent and children managed independently (useful for loose coupling)
  • Hybrid Approach: Some children included, others referenced externally

Example: Disk Controller with Drives 

// Inclusive approach - controller includes drive configurations
{
  "controllerSettings": { "motorSpeed": "normal" },
  "drives": [
    { "slot": 0, "imagePath": "System.dsk", "writeProtected": true },
    { "slot": 1, "imagePath": "Data.dsk", "writeProtected": false }
  ]
}

// When applying metadata, the controller:
// 1. Reads its own settings
// 2. Creates/configures child drives based on embedded configuration
// 3. Recursively applies metadata to each child

This approach keeps the configuration self-contained and ensures all related state travels together. The parent is responsible for creating and initializing its children during ApplyMetadata(string).

Implementation Guidelines:

  1. GetMetadata() should never throw exceptions (return empty string on error)
  2. ApplyMetadata(string) should validate input and return false on failure
  3. Metadata format should be documented in implementing class remarks
  4. Consider including version information in metadata for backward compatibility
  5. Empty string metadata should represent default/unconfigured state
  6. For hierarchical objects, decide whether children are included or referenced
  7. Document the hierarchical strategy in the implementing class

Methods

ApplyMetadata(string)

Applies configuration metadata to this object, restoring its state.

bool ApplyMetadata(string metadata)

Parameters

metadata string

A metadata string previously obtained from GetMetadata(), or an empty string to reset to default configuration.

Returns

bool

true if the metadata was successfully parsed and applied; false if the metadata was invalid, incompatible, or could not be applied for any reason.

Examples

// Successful application
var device = new Device();
if (device.ApplyMetadata("{\"name\":\"COM1\",\"baud\":9600}"))
{
    Console.WriteLine("Configuration applied successfully");
}

// Reset to defaults
if (device.ApplyMetadata(string.Empty))
{
    Console.WriteLine("Reset to default configuration");
}

// Handle failure
if (!device.ApplyMetadata("invalid metadata"))
{
    Console.WriteLine("Failed to apply configuration");
}

// Hierarchical application
var controller = new DiskController();
string metadata = @"{
    ""drives"": [
        { ""slot"": 0, ""driveConfig"": ""{...}"" },
        { ""slot"": 1, ""driveConfig"": ""{...}"" }
    ]
}";

if (controller.ApplyMetadata(metadata))
{
    // Controller and all drives configured successfully
}

Remarks

This method attempts to restore the object's configuration state from a metadata string. The method should validate the metadata format and contents before applying any changes. If validation fails at any point, the method should return false and leave the object in a consistent state (preferably unchanged).

Empty String Handling:
Passing an empty or whitespace-only string should reset the object to its default configuration state and return true. This provides a standard way to clear configuration without needing format-specific knowledge.

Hierarchical Configuration:
For objects with child components, ApplyMetadata(string) is responsible for:

  1. Parsing the parent's configuration from metadata
  2. Creating or locating child objects based on metadata
  3. Recursively applying embedded child metadata to each child
  4. Validating the entire configuration hierarchy

If any child fails to apply its metadata, the parent should return false and either roll back all changes or leave the object in a consistent partial state (documented by the implementing class).

Common Failure Scenarios:

  • Format errors: Malformed JSON/XML, invalid syntax
  • Schema mismatch: Missing required fields, unknown fields
  • Version incompatibility: Metadata from newer version
  • Resource errors: Referenced files don't exist, resources unavailable
  • Validation errors: Values out of range, invalid combinations
  • Child configuration failure: Child object failed to apply its embedded metadata

Partial Application:
Implementations should apply changes atomically when possible. If metadata contains multiple settings and one fails validation, the implementation should either apply none of them (transactional) or document which settings were applied (partial). The return value indicates overall success/failure.

Error Handling:
This method should never throw exceptions. All errors should result in returning false. Implementations may log errors for diagnostic purposes.

GetMetadata()

Gets the current configuration state as a metadata string.

string GetMetadata()

Returns

string

A string containing serialized configuration data in an implementation-specific format, or an empty string if the object has no configuration state or if serialization fails.

Remarks

This method captures the current configuration state of the object in a format suitable for persistence, transmission, or cloning. The format is entirely up to the implementer and should be documented in the implementing class.

Format Examples:

  • Simple objects: JSON or key-value pairs
  • Complex objects: YAML, XML, or custom formats
  • Binary data: Base64-encoded binary blob
  • No state: Empty string
  • Hierarchical objects: Nested structures with child configurations embedded

Hierarchical Objects:
Objects with child components that also implement IConfigurable may choose to:

  • Embed child metadata within the parent's metadata (keeps configuration self-contained)
  • Reference children externally (allows independent child management)
  • Use a hybrid approach based on business logic requirements

The choice depends on the relationship between parent and child—tightly coupled components (like a controller and its drives) typically use embedded metadata, while loosely coupled components might prefer external references.

Error Handling:
This method should never throw exceptions. If serialization fails for any reason, return an empty string. The caller can choose to treat empty string as "no configuration" or as an error condition.

Thread Safety:
Implementations should ensure this method is safe to call from any thread, though concurrent modification during serialization may produce inconsistent results.

See Also

ICard
